map.condition: Checking if a function is continuous at a point
Limit of (sin x)/x type as x→0

map.use: f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a} \text{ for some } c \in (a,b)

formulas.name: Trigonometric Limit: sin x / x
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever a sin(x)/x or equivalent form appears as x approaches 0.
formulas.trap: The angle must be in radians; in degrees the limit is π/180, not 1.

formulas.name: Trigonometric Limit: tan x / x
formulas.whenToUse: Direct evaluation of tan(x)/x as x → 0.
formulas.trap: Does not equal 1 for x → ∞; confuse with sin/x only at 0.

formulas.name: Limit: (1 - cos x) / x²
formulas.whenToUse: Cosine-based 0/0 limits near the origin.
formulas.trap: Forgetting the 1/2 factor; (1-cos x)/x → 0, not 1/2.

formulas.name: Exponential Limit: (1 + 1/n)^n
formulas.whenToUse: Limits of the form (1 + f(x))^{1/f(x)} as f(x)→0.
formulas.trap: Must be in the form (1 + 1/n)^n exactly; (1 + 2/n)^n = e² not e.

formulas.name: Logarithmic Limit: ln(1+x)/x
formulas.whenToUse: Logarithm-based 0/0 indeterminate forms near x = 0.
formulas.trap: Only valid for ln (base e); for log base 10 the limit is log₁₀(e) ≈ 0.434.

formulas.name: L'Hôpital's Rule
formulas.plain: lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x) when 0/0 or inf/inf
formulas.whenToUse: Indeterminate forms 0/0 or ∞/∞ where direct substitution fails.
formulas.trap: Applies only to 0/0 or ∞/∞; must convert other forms (0·∞, 1^∞, etc.) first.

formulas.name: Continuity at a Point
formulas.latex: f \text{ is continuous at } a \iff \lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = f(a)
formulas.plain: f continuous at a iff LHL = RHL = f(a)
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying or disproving continuity at a specific point.

terms.means: value of the function at a (must be defined)

formulas.trap: All three conditions must hold; limit existing without f(a) being defined is NOT continuity.

formulas.name: Types of Discontinuity
formulas.latex: \text{Removable: } \lim_{x\to a} f(x) \text{ exists but} \neq f(a); \quad \text{Jump: } L^- \neq L^+; \quad \text{Infinite: } |f(x)| \to \infty
formulas.plain: Removable: limit exists ≠ f(a); Jump: LHL ≠ RHL; Infinite: |f|→∞
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying the nature of a discontinuity.
formulas.trap: A jump discontinuity is NOT removable even if both one-sided limits exist.

formulas.name: Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
formulas.latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b],\; f(a) \neq f(b) \Rightarrow \forall k \in (f(a),f(b)),\; \exists c \in (a,b): f(c)=k
formulas.plain: If f continuous on [a,b] and f(a)≠f(b), then every value between f(a) and f(b) is attained.
formulas.whenToUse: Existence of roots or intermediate values of continuous functions.

terms.means: any value strictly between f(a) and f(b)
the guaranteed point in the open interval

formulas.trap: IVT guarantees existence, NOT uniqueness of c.

formulas.name: Definition of Derivative (First Principles)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing derivatives from first principles or checking differentiability at a point.
formulas.trap: The limit must exist (be finite and equal from both sides) for differentiability; existence of limit ≠ continuous derivative everywhere.

formulas.name: Differentiability Implies Continuity
formulas.latex: f \text{ differentiable at } a \Rightarrow f \text{ continuous at } a \quad (\text{converse is FALSE})
formulas.plain: Differentiable => Continuous, but Continuous does NOT => Differentiable
formulas.whenToUse: Reasoning about the relationship between the two properties.
formulas.trap: |x| is continuous at 0 but NOT differentiable there — classic counter-example.

formulas.name: Left-Hand and Right-Hand Derivatives
formulas.whenToUse: Checking differentiability of piecewise-defined functions at join points.
formulas.trap: LHD = RHD is necessary AND sufficient for differentiability at a point.

formulas.name: Chain Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Differentiating composite functions.

terms.means: composite of outer f and inner g

formulas.trap: Must differentiate the outer function evaluated at the inner, then multiply by derivative of inner.

formulas.name: Product Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Differentiating a product of two functions.
formulas.trap: Students sometimes write (uv)' = u'v'; the second term uv' is also required.

formulas.name: Quotient Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Differentiating a ratio of two functions.
formulas.trap: Order matters: u'v − uv', NOT uv' − u'v (sign error flips the answer).

formulas.name: Standard Derivatives Table
formulas.latex: \frac{d}{dx} x^n = nx^{n-1},\quad \frac{d}{dx} e^x = e^x,\quad \frac{d}{dx} \ln x = \frac{1}{x},\quad \frac{d}{dx} \sin x = \cos x,\quad \frac{d}{dx} \cos x = -\sin x
formulas.plain: d/dx x^n = nx^(n-1); d/dx e^x = e^x; d/dx ln x = 1/x; d/dx sin x = cos x; d/dx cos x = -sin x
formulas.whenToUse: Quick reference for elementary function derivatives.
formulas.trap: d/dx cos x = −sin x (negative sign); many students drop it under time pressure.

formulas.name: Rolle's Theorem
formulas.latex: f \text{ cont. on } [a,b],\; \text{diff. on } (a,b),\; f(a)=f(b) \Rightarrow \exists c \in (a,b): f'(c)=0
formulas.plain: If f continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), f(a)=f(b), then f'(c)=0 for some c in (a,b).
formulas.whenToUse: Proving existence of a point with zero derivative; basis for MVT.
formulas.trap: All three conditions are necessary; dropping any one invalidates the conclusion.

formulas.name: Mean Value Theorem (Lagrange's MVT)
formulas.latex: f \text{ cont. on } [a,b],\; \text{diff. on } (a,b) \Rightarrow \exists c \in (a,b): f'(c) = \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding function change, proving inequalities, and existence arguments.
formulas.trap: MVT gives existence of c but NOT its exact value; also c ∈ (a,b) (open interval).

formulas.name: Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem
formulas.whenToUse: Generalization of MVT involving two functions; basis for L'Hôpital's proof.
formulas.trap: Requires g'(c) ≠ 0 and g(a) ≠ g(b); reduces to Lagrange's MVT when g(x)=x.

formulas.name: First Derivative Test
formulas.latex: f'(a) = 0;\; f' \text{ changes } + \to - \Rightarrow \text{local max};\; f' \text{ changes } - \to + \Rightarrow \text{local min}
formulas.plain: f'(a)=0; f' goes +→- means local max; f' goes -→+ means local min
formulas.whenToUse: Finding local extrema by sign change of the first derivative.

terms.means: critical point where f'(a)=0 or undefined

formulas.trap: f'(a)=0 alone does NOT guarantee an extremum (e.g., f(x)=x³ at x=0 is an inflection point).

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test
formulas.latex: f'(a)=0 \text{ and } f''(a) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{local max}; \quad f'(a)=0 \text{ and } f''(a) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{local min}
formulas.plain: f'(a)=0 and f''(a)<0 => local max; f'(a)=0 and f''(a)>0 => local min
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly classifying critical points without tracing sign of f'.

terms.means: second derivative at the critical point

formulas.trap: If f''(a)=0 the test is INCONCLUSIVE; must revert to first derivative test or higher derivatives.

formulas.name: Monotonicity Conditions
formulas.latex: f'(x) > 0 \; \forall x \in I \Rightarrow f \text{ strictly increasing on } I; \quad f'(x) < 0 \Rightarrow f \text{ strictly decreasing on } I
formulas.plain: f'(x)>0 on I => strictly increasing; f'(x)<0 on I => strictly decreasing
formulas.whenToUse: Determining intervals of increase/decrease.
formulas.trap: f'(x)=0 at isolated points still allows strict monotonicity on the interval.
